title: The Future of AI is Hyperconnected - Paid Program - WSJ
full_text:
Today, most companies are realizing Generative AI (GenAI) can do more than enhance low-value tasks; when used strategically, it will offer businesses a competitive edge. Predicted to contribute $4.4 trillion to the global economy, GenAI has the potential to revolutionize how we work across functions, from sales and marketing to software development and R&D . Since ChatGPT’s primetime debut, business leaders have started recognizing the technology’s applicability—and crucially—accept that ignoring GenAI poses substantial risks to their growth.
In a recent global report published by MIT Technology Review Insights in partnership with Telstra International, which surveyed senior executives on their attitudes toward GenAI, a majority of respondents (78%) recognized the technology as a competitive opportunity. Almost two-thirds said they were actively looking to unlock hidden opportunities from their data, yet around the same number admitted to currently deploying the technology only in a limited fashion. Despite enthusiasm for applying GenAI solutions across business functions, practical barriers such as connectivity may hinder implementation.
Connecting Data in the AI Era
As GenAI continues to evolve, its influence may extend to cross-industry workflows and practices—from optimizing logistics and supply chains to transforming healthcare delivery and public services. Still, the successful adoption of GenAI is a multifaceted challenge for most organizations. At its core, it requires innovation to drive the effective and efficient use of data for business value. Put another way, AI is driving investment in machine-to-machine (M2M) and human-to-machine (H2M) digital interactions, which require organizations to capture, process and contextualize vast amounts of data to make informed business decisions in real-time.
Robust connectivity is essential for corporate decision-makers seeking to unleash GenAI’s full potential. Beyond static network linkages, enterprises need to build a seamless ecosystem where devices, processes and people are pervasively and persistently interconnected.
Telstra International, a leading global telecommunications and technology company, defines this connectivity strategy as “hyperconnected digital fabrics”. Underlying technologies like 5G, IoT, edge computing and the cloud streamline the use of data across distributed and disparate platforms, which is fundamental to running AI applications effectively in most corporate environments.
“The digital fabric is the technological foundation for businesses to unlock value within their ecosystems using shared data, applications and operations,” says Linus Lai, chief analyst and vice president for Digital Business, Trust and Services at IDC Australia and New Zealand. He affirms that only by achieving this collaborative efficiency can data-driven entities truly realize their competitive advantage.
Omni-Channel Connectivity
The interconnectedness of distributed systems, edges and locations—inside and outside corporate boundaries—critically influences how data is transmitted and processed within the digital fabric.
“In the era of hyperconnected businesses and AI, leveraging value chains within and beyond corporate boundaries is paramount to gaining a competitive edge,” says Geraldine Kor, Managing Director of South Asia and Head of Global Enterprise at Telstra International. “The digital fabric enables the smooth and secure flow of data throughout an organization and the external vertical ecosystems in which it operates, allowing digital services to generate more business value and help companies gain market share.”
To successfully exploit the AI opportunity, companies must excel in handling and harnessing data to make informed decisions in real-time or near real-time .
Geraldine Kor, Managing Director of South Asia and Head of Global Enterprise, Telstra International
E-commerce ecosystems, for instance, can consist of several cross-industry participants, including customers, suppliers, financial service providers and logistics partners. This omni-channel ordering, payments and distribution ecosystem drives high-volume exchanges of data, which boosts sales through an integrated end-customer experience in verticals like retail and manufacturing. With consumer behavior data, companies can continue innovating personalized shopping experiences and engage in a virtuous cycle of value creation.
In another example, digital fabric-enabled connectivity enables real-time data sharing, which empowers manufacturing leaders to design market-leading strategies, enhance operational scale and build automated and predictive supply chain agility. “Harnessing data in motion from the digital fabric improves supply chain efficiencies and enables ecosystem-based models to counter rapid changes in the marketplace,” says IDC’s Lai.
In the context of Asia Pacific’s fast-growing digital economies, companies are adopting AI and hyperconnectivity to sharpen their competitiveness. Four-in-five Singaporean e-commerce companies already use artificial intelligence as a tool for business growth, according to a recent survey of e-commerce practitioners in 15 countries in the region.
Scaling Up
Prioritizing connectivity is a significant challenge in itself. IT decision-makers must consider the latency-sensitivity of modern applications, the scalability of computing infrastructure, regulatory requirements and the needs of decentralized workforces. These burdens are complicated by the potential economic costs and security risks of storing, processing and transmitting sensitive data.
Companies looking to scale up their AI infrastructure and services—especially in regions experiencing rapid urbanization—can benefit from a technology partner that understands the business ROI of digital fabric connectivity. Telstra International has Asia Pacific’s largest wholly-owned subsea cable network, which carries over a third of the region’s internet traffic and offers access to over 2,000 points of presence in more than 200 countries and territories. And through its technology services arm Telstra Purple, Telstra International delivers AI and Data envisioning workshops to help organizations begin and accelerate their AI adoption journey.
Additionally, Telstra provides comprehensive managed security services via its state-of-the-art Security Operations Centers in Australia, complemented by service centers in Asia and the U.K. Their integrated offering encompasses physical infrastructure, software-driven services and business capabilities, creating a robust platform for AI-enabled innovation and growth.
According to Telstra International’s Kor, this platform can be a crucial stepping stone to making AI work for enterprise. “To successfully exploit the AI opportunity, companies must excel in handling and harnessing data to make informed decisions in real-time or near real-time,” she says. “This will set them apart from others in this new competitive environment.”

# The Future of AI is Hyperconnected - Paid Program - WSJ

- raw_id: R-037

## clean_text

Today, most companies are realizing Generative AI (GenAI) can do more than enhance low-value tasks; when used strategically, it will offer businesses a competitive edge. Predicted to contribute $4.4 trillion to the global economy, GenAI has the potential to revolutionize how we work across functions, from sales and marketing to software development and R&D . Since ChatGPT’s primetime debut, business leaders have started recognizing the technology’s applicability—and crucially—accept that ignoring GenAI poses substantial risks to their growth.
In a recent global report published by MIT Technology Review Insights in partnership with Telstra International, which surveyed senior executives on their attitudes toward GenAI, a majority of respondents (78%) recognized the technology as a competitive opportunity. Almost two-thirds said they were actively looking to unlock hidden opportunities from their data, yet around the same number admitted to currently deploying the technology only in a limited fashion. Despite enthusiasm for applying GenAI solutions across business functions, practical barriers such as connectivity may hinder implementation.
Connecting Data in the AI Era
As GenAI continues to evolve, its influence may extend to cross-industry workflows and practices—from optimizing logistics and supply chains to transforming healthcare delivery and public services. Still, the successful adoption of GenAI is a multifaceted challenge for most organizations. At its core, it requires innovation to drive the effective and efficient use of data for business value. Put another way, AI is driving investment in machine-to-machine (M2M) and human-to-machine (H2M) digital interactions, which require organizations to capture, process and contextualize vast amounts of data to make informed business decisions in real-time.
Robust connectivity is essential for corporate decision-makers seeking to unleash GenAI’s full potential. Beyond static network linkages, enterprises need to build a seamless ecosystem where devices, processes and people are pervasively and persistently interconnected.
Telstra International, a leading global telecommunications and technology company, defines this connectivity strategy as “hyperconnected digital fabrics”. Underlying technologies like 5G, IoT, edge computing and the cloud streamline the use of data across distributed and disparate platforms, which is fundamental to running AI applications effectively in most corporate environments.
“The digital fabric is the technological foundation for businesses to unlock value within their ecosystems using shared data, applications and operations,” says Linus Lai, chief analyst and vice president for Digital Business, Trust and Services at IDC Australia and New Zealand. He affirms that only by achieving this collaborative efficiency can data-driven entities truly realize their competitive advantage.
Omni-Channel Connectivity
The interconnectedness of distributed systems, edges and locations—inside and outside corporate boundaries—critically influences how data is transmitted and processed within the digital fabric.
“In the era of hyperconnected businesses and AI, leveraging value chains within and beyond corporate boundaries is paramount to gaining a competitive edge,” says Geraldine Kor, Managing Director of South Asia and Head of Global Enterprise at Telstra International. “The digital fabric enables the smooth and secure flow of data throughout an organization and the external vertical ecosystems in which it operates, allowing digital services to generate more business value and help companies gain market share.”
To successfully exploit the AI opportunity, companies must excel in handling and harnessing data to make informed decisions in real-time or near real-time .
Geraldine Kor, Managing Director of South Asia and Head of Global Enterprise, Telstra International
E-commerce ecosystems, for instance, can consist of several cross-industry participants, including customers, suppliers, financial service providers and logistics partners. This omni-channel ordering, payments and distribution ecosystem drives high-volume exchanges of data, which boosts sales through an integrated end-customer experience in verticals like retail and manufacturing. With consumer behavior data, companies can continue innovating personalized shopping experiences and engage in a virtuous cycle of value creation.
In another example, digital fabric-enabled connectivity enables real-time data sharing, which empowers manufacturing leaders to design market-leading strategies, enhance operational scale and build automated and predictive supply chain agility. “Harnessing data in motion from the digital fabric improves supply chain efficiencies and enables ecosystem-based models to counter rapid changes in the marketplace,” says IDC’s Lai.
In the context of Asia Pacific’s fast-growing digital economies, companies are adopting AI and hyperconnectivity to sharpen their competitiveness. Four-in-five Singaporean e-commerce companies already use artificial intelligence as a tool for business growth, according to a recent survey of e-commerce practitioners in 15 countries in the region.
Scaling Up
Prioritizing connectivity is a significant challenge in itself. IT decision-makers must consider the latency-sensitivity of modern applications, the scalability of computing infrastructure, regulatory requirements and the needs of decentralized workforces. These burdens are complicated by the potential economic costs and security risks of storing, processing and transmitting sensitive data.
Companies looking to scale up their AI infrastructure and services—especially in regions experiencing rapid urbanization—can benefit from a technology partner that understands the business ROI of digital fabric connectivity. Telstra International has Asia Pacific’s largest wholly-owned subsea cable network, which carries over a third of the region’s internet traffic and offers access to over 2,000 points of presence in more than 200 countries and territories. And through its technology services arm Telstra Purple, Telstra International delivers AI and Data envisioning workshops to help organizations begin and accelerate their AI adoption journey.
Additionally, Telstra provides comprehensive managed security services via its state-of-the-art Security Operations Centers in Australia, complemented by service centers in Asia and the U.K. Their integrated offering encompasses physical infrastructure, software-driven services and business capabilities, creating a robust platform for AI-enabled innovation and growth.
According to Telstra International’s Kor, this platform can be a crucial stepping stone to making AI work for enterprise. “To successfully exploit the AI opportunity, companies must excel in handling and harnessing data to make informed decisions in real-time or near real-time,” she says. “This will set them apart from others in this new competitive environment.”
